entry.title: Welcome to Academic Partnerships in Latin America, the Caribbean, Spain, and Portugal!
entry.content:
Welcome to Academic Partnerships in Latin America, the Caribbean, Spain, and Portugal!


It is a pleasure to have you here and we hope you have a great experience with us. Our goal is to connect and engage with all University Alliances program initiatives, so let’s get to know who we are and what we do here.

Who Are We?

The SAP University Alliances program is a key part of SAP’s strategy to develop future talent. It is an external initiative that supports universities in educating the next generation by enabling students to learn, research, and innovate using real-world business applications. Through this program, academia can prepare students for the intelligent enterprise and the experience economy, participate in SAP-led events, build industry partnerships, and equip graduates with the skills needed to thrive in the global SAP ecosystem.

The SAP University Alliances is an external program managed by the Academic Partnerships team.

The Academic Partnerships team is represented across Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC), Spain, and Portugal by María Cecilia Calzada de Neumann, Director for Iberoamérica and Isadora Royer, SAP Campus Ambassadors Lead for the Americas. They are supported by a team of interns who contribute to and help drive the program’s initiatives.

 

 

What Are the Advantages and Benefits of Being Part of the SAP University Alliances Program?

  • Integrate SAP solutions into university curricula, including undergraduate theses and academic projects. As a global leader in business applications, SAP allows universities to incorporate best practices, learning content, and state-of-the-art training platforms for programs in Technology, Business, Engineering, and many other disciplines.
  • Become part of an international academic community with more than 2,800 institutions across 100+ countries, with multiple global events and opportunities for collaboration and networking.
  • Create a hub for co-innovation, offering benefits for universities, faculties, and students through connections with local companies, government institutions, accelerators, startups, and the broader innovation ecosystem.
  • Improve graduate employability, facilitating students’ integration into the SAP ecosystem within local and global markets.

Free Resources & Opportunities 

Through the SAP University Alliances program, students and educators can access a wide range of free learning resources and teaching materials designed to bridge the gap between theory and practice. Explore the full portfolio of teaching and learning offerings on SAP University Alliances page.

What you can expect:

Learning Platforms & Hands-On Experience
  • SAP Learning Hub, student edition (free for students and professors) 
    • Free access to courses, learning journeys, and live sessions
    • Hands-on practice systems
    • Two certification attempts per year
  • Business Builders
    • Gamified learning experience using SAP Analytics Cloud
    • Focus on storytelling, analytics, and decision-making skills
    • Designed to simulate real-world business scenarios
Teaching Content & Academic Materials
    • Learning Journeys
    • Ready-to-use resources to support course integration
    • Structured content to guide curriculum design
    • Materials aligned with real-world business applications
Community, Training & Enablement
  • Train-the-Trainer programs and bootcamps
  • Participation in SAP events, workshops and special sessions
  • Opportunities to connect with a global academic network

 

Full Membership – UCC Chico (LAC)

For institutions seeking a more comprehensive and hands-on teaching environment, SAP University Alliances offers Full Membership options through University Competence Centers (UCCs).

This model provides access to live SAP systems, full curricula, and technical support, enabling universities to deliver highly practical, system-based learning experiences. Universities can choose from different packages depending on their teaching needs. Includes everything from the free offering level plus:

Two contracts:

  • One between SAP and the educational institution for academic software licenses (free).
  • One with the University Competence Center (UCC) for hosting and technical support (paid).

Annual investment for hosting and 24/7 support of SAP solutions through California State University, Chico (SAP’s UCC for LAC).

Institutions can select from a range of packages tailored to their curriculum and scale, offering access to UCC-hosted learning environments and SAP solutions, starting from USD 3,000 per year.

  • In SAP S/4HANA, one client can provide access for up to nearly 1,000 users, including professors.
  • A single ERP client can support multiple courses and disciplines.
  • Students gain access to the software during the academic period for classroom use.

Additional Cloud Options

Institutions can also expand their setup with additional single cloud environments, depending on their curriculum focus.

Available solutions include:

  • SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)
  • SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP)
  • SAP Datasphere
  • SAP Business ByDesign
  • SAP Ariba
  • SAP SuccessFactors
  • SAP BTP (including Pro Code & Integration Suite)
  • SAP Business One

Flexibility & Academic Use

A single environment can support multiple courses and disciplines, such as:

    • ERP
    • Analytics
    • Supply Chain Management
    • Finance
    • Artificial Intelligence

Example SAP solutions available through UCC Chico hosting include:

SAP S/4HANA – Global Bike

A widely used academic scenario that teaches how fundamental business processes interact with key SAP ERP modules such as:

  • Sales and Distribution
  • Materials Management
  • Production Planning
  • Financial and Controlling Accounting
  • Human Capital Management
  • Warehouse Management
  • And many others.

Note: UCC Chico supports only Latin America and the Caribbean. For Spain and Portugal, universities should explore the options offered by UCC TUM.

At its core, SAP University Alliances is about creating opportunities: connecting students, educators and institutions to the tools, technologies, and networks that shape the future of work.

Through a combination of free resources, collaborative initiatives, and hands-on learning experiences, the program empowers academia to go beyond theory and deliver real-world impact. Whether you are integrating SAP into your curriculum, exploring new teaching approaches, or developing your own skills, there are multiple ways to get involved.

We invite you to explore our official SAP University Alliances official page, discover available resources, and take the next step in your journey with the SAP University Alliances program.

entry.title: Configuration Differences When SAP Datasphere Is Integrated into SAP BDC vs. Standalone
entry.content:
Initially, the landscape was as follows:
ECC → SAP LT → SAP Cloud Connector → SAP Datasphere → Azure Data Lake

However, the landscape has now changed to:
ECC → SAP LT → SAP Cloud Connector → SAP Datasphere (integrated into SAP BDC) → Azure Data Lake

Question:
Are there any configuration differences in SAP Cloud Connector, SAP Datasphere (integrated into SAP BDC), and Azure Data Lake compared to the setup when using SAP Datasphere as a standalone system?

entry.title: Secure SAP SuccessFactors APIs with SAP API Management and Microsoft Entra Token Exchange in IAS
entry.content:
April, 2026  |  SAP Integration Suite & Security

Introduction

Enterprise organizations frequently operate in multi-cloud environments where Microsoft Azure — and specifically Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) — serves as the primary identity provider. At the same time, SAP landscapes rely on SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) as the trusted token authority for SAP applications.

When these worlds need to meet at an API boundary — for example, a custom application or integration runtime managed in Azure that must consume SAP SuccessFactors APIs — a common question arises: how do you bridge identity without compromising security or creating brittle, hard-to-maintain workarounds?

This post walks through a standardized pattern that solves exactly this problem. Using SAP Integration Suite – API Management as the enforcement point, SAP Identity Authentication (IAS) as the canonical token issuer, and OAuth 2.0 JWT Bearer Token Exchange (RFC 7523) as the bridge mechanism, API consumers can present a native Microsoft Entra ID token and receive seamless, secure access to SAP SuccessFactors OData and REST APIs — with no custom identity mapping code required.

In scope: This pattern covers machine-to-machine (M2M) API access using the client_credentials grant type. The approach is equally applicable to service integrations, middleware, and automated data pipelines operating under a service principal identity registered in Microsoft Entra ID.

The Challenge: Two Identity Worlds, One API Gateway

SAP SuccessFactors enforces that API access tokens must be issued by a trusted identity provider. Historically, this meant developers either had to maintain separate credential sets within the SAP ecosystem or implement custom token-translation logic inside their integration middleware.

Neither approach scales well. Separate credentials introduce operational overhead and increase the attack surface. Custom token-exchange code is fragile, difficult to audit, and tightly coupled to specific implementation details.

The ideal solution standardizes identity at the API gateway layer, ensuring that:

  • API consumers authenticate against the identity provider they already trust — Microsoft Entra ID.
  • The gateway handles token exchange transparently, with no changes required in client applications.
  • SAP SuccessFactors receives only IAS-issued tokens, preserving SAP's trust model.
  • All token validation and exchange follows open, auditable standards (OAuth 2.0, OIDC, RFC 7523).

Solution Overview

The pattern uses SAP API Management as the central brokering point. Inbound API calls carry a Microsoft Entra ID JWT token. The API Management policy pipeline intercepts this token, exchanges it for an IAS-issued access token via a ServiceCallout policy, then forwards the IAS token to SAP SuccessFactors. The SuccessFactors API endpoint validates the IAS token and returns the requested data.

Component Role in the Pattern

Microsoft Entra IDIssues the initial JWT access token to the API consumer (service principal / app registration).
API ConsumerAny client application that holds an Entra ID token.
SAP API ManagementPerforms the JWT Bearer Token Exchange, and routes the call to SuccessFactors with the IAS token.
SAP Identity AuthenticationAccepts the Entra JWT, validates it, and issues an IAS access token scoped for SuccessFactors.
SAP SuccessFactorsTarget API. Accepts only IAS-issued tokens (as per SAP Note 3681224).

Architecture Diagram

Figure 1 — End-to-end authentication flow: API Consumer → Microsoft Entra ID → SAP API Management → SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) → SAP SuccessFactors

The diagram illustrates the trust relationships and communication paths:

  • Azure side: An app registration in Microsoft Entra ID issues access_token JWTs to the API consumer under the client_credentials grant type.
  • SAP BTP side: Within SAP Cloud  identity Service - Identity Authentication (IAS) an IAS application representing SAP API Management is configured with Entra ID as a trusted (corporate) identity provider. A dependency link maps this IAS application to the IAS SuccessFactors application, which holds the scopes for SF APIs.
  • SuccessFactors side: Create technical user with authorization (RBAC) according to the resources exposed in API. User Name should be the same as subject claim (Entra ID service principal). To avoid user claims in the IAS access token, the technical user should not be synchronized, created in IAS.

Step-by-Step Authentication Flow

  1. Client acquires a Microsoft Entra ID access token.
    The API consumer authenticates to Microsoft Entra ID using the client_credentials grant type and receives a signed JWT.
    # Token request to Microsoft Entra ID
POST https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant-id}/oauth2/v2.0/token

client_id     = {{ENTRAClientid}}
client_secret = {{ENTRASecret}}
grant_type    = client_credentials
scope         = {{scope}}
  2. Client calls the SAP API Management endpoint with the Entra token.
    The Entra JWT is presented in the standard Authorization: Bearer header. The API Management proxy receives the request and begins policy evaluation.
    # Inbound API call to SAP API Management
GET https://{apim-host}/api/{resource-path}
Authorization: Bearer {{EntraAccessToken}}
  3. SAP API Management performs JWT Bearer Token Exchange (RFC 7523) via IAS.
    A ServiceCallout policy in the API proxy calls the IAS token endpoint. The Entra token is passed as the assertion parameter. The resource parameter targets the IAS dependency that represents the SuccessFactors application.
    # Token exchange request from APIM to IAS
POST https://{ias-tenant}.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token

client_id     = {{IASClientid}}
client_secret = {{IASSecret}}
grant_type    = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer
assertion     = {{EntraAccessToken}}
resource      = urn:sap:identity:application:provider:name:{{dependency}}
  4. SAP IAS validates the Entra JWT and issues a SuccessFactors-scoped IAS token.
    IAS validates the inbound Entra JWT per RFC 7523 criteria (signature, issuer, audience, expiry). Because Microsoft Entra ID is configured as a trusted corporate identity provider in the IAS application, and the dependency link to the SuccessFactors IAS app is in place, IAS issues a new access token scoped for SuccessFactors. If validation fails, IAS returns an OAuth 2.0 error response (RFC 6749).
  5. SAP API Management forwards the call to SAP SuccessFactors with the IAS token.
    The API proxy replaces the inbound Entra token with the IAS access token and routes the request to the SuccessFactors OData or REST API endpoint. SuccessFactors validates the IAS token and returns the requested data.
    # Outbound API call from APIM to SuccessFactors
GET https://{sf-api-host}/odata/v2/{entity-set}
Authorization: Bearer {{IASAccessToken}}

Key Configuration Prerequisites

1. SAP Identity Authentication (IAS) — Corporate IdP Setup

In the IAS administration console, configure Microsoft Entra ID as a Corporate Identity Provider and set it as the Default Identity Provider under Conditional Authentication for the SAP API Management application. This establishes the trust chain that allows IAS to accept Entra-issued JWTs during the RFC 7523 exchange.

2. IAS Application — Dependency Configuration

Two IAS application registrations are required:

  • IAS SAP API Management app — This is the application that the ServiceCallout authenticates against. It must have
    • Grant Type: JWT Bearer
    • Trusted token issuer: Entra ID
    • Dependency: e.g. APIM2SF (pointing to the IAS SuccessFactors app)
  • IAS SuccessFactors app — Holds the OAuth scopes for SuccessFactors APIs and is referenced via the dependency link.

3. SAP SuccessFactors — Accept IAS-Issued Tokens

Important: SAP SuccessFactors must be explicitly configured to trust and accept IAS-issued tokens. This is a prerequisite documented in SAP Note 3681224. Review and apply part 1 of this note before deploying the pattern.

4. SAP API Management — Policy Configuration

The API proxy in SAP API Management must implement two policy steps:

  1. A ServiceCallout policy to call the IAS token endpoint with the RFC 7523 parameters described above.
  2. A Set Authorization: Bearer policy to replace the inbound Entra token with the IAS token before routing to SuccessFactors.

Why This Pattern Works — Key Design Principles

This design is effective because it respects the trust model of each platform without forcing either side to accept a foreign token directly:

  • No credential sharing across platforms. Client applications never need SAP-specific credentials. They authenticate exclusively with Entra ID using their existing service principal.
  • Standards-based token exchange. RFC 7523 is a well-specified IETF standard. Using it means the exchange logic is predictable, auditable, and interoperable.
  • Centralized policy enforcement. SAP API Management is the single enforcement and mediation point. Token exchange logic lives in the gateway policy, not scattered across individual client applications.
  • Transparent to API consumers. Client applications are completely unaware that a token exchange occurs. They present an Entra token and receive SuccessFactors data — the mediation is entirely internal to the gateway.
  • SAP's trust model is preserved. SuccessFactors only ever receives IAS-issued tokens, maintaining the integrity of the SAP identity trust chain.

Relevant Standards and References

Reference Description

RFC 7523JSON Web Token (JWT) Profile for OAuth 2.0 Client Authentication and Authorization Grants — defines the urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer grant type used for the token exchange.
RFC 6749The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework — baseline specification for token request/response formats, including error responses.
OIDCIdentity layer on top of OAuth 2.0. Microsoft Entra ID issues OIDC-compliant JWTs; IAS supports OIDC for trust federation.
SAP Note 3681224SAP Identity Authentication (IAS) configuration to enable generation of IAS-issued access tokens. Part 1 - Must be applied before this pattern can be used. Part 2  - Manage OIDC OAuth Client Application is not needed in this scenario!

Conclusion

The JWT Bearer Token Exchange pattern described here provides a clean, standards-based path for enterprises running Microsoft Entra ID as their primary identity provider to consume SAP SuccessFactors APIs through SAP API Management — without compromising on security, maintainability, or SAP's identity trust model.

By centralizing the token exchange in SAP API Management and leveraging the proven RFC 7523 grant type, organizations can:

  • Onboard new API consumers rapidly, using only their existing Entra ID service principal.
  • Restrict authorizations per API consumers (Entra ID service principal) via RBAC in SAP SuccessFactors. 
  • Enforce consistent security policies at a single gateway layer.
  • Avoid brittle, platform-specific identity workarounds.
  • Maintain a clear audit trail of token issuance and exchange across both identity systems.

If your organization is evaluating multi-cloud API security patterns for SAP workloads, this approach is worth considering as part of your broader integration suite strategy.

Related resources:
SAP Note 3681224 — SAP SuccessFactors: Enabling IAS token acceptance
SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication documentation
SAP API Management documentation
IETF RFC 7523 — JWT Profile for OAuth 2.0

entry.title: Re: Standard Field does not appear in BAPICCBVB BAPI_COND...
entry.content:
Hi @HendryTj, 

Even though BWART is a standard field in WCB_V_MKPF_MSEG, that's not a standard field in the field catalog for Business volume selection criteria. 

To use this field via the BAPI, you’ll need to leverage the EXTENSIONIN parameter:

  1. Append structure BAPI_TE_BVB
    • Add the field BWART to this structure.

  2. Pass the data via EXTENSIONIN
    • Populate BAPI_TE_BVB with all required fields, including:
      • The Order Key (linked to your BVB field combination)
      • The Condition Contract number (mandatory for change BAPI, optional for create)
      • The BVB GUID
      • Your custom field BWART

This is the standard way to handle fields not exposed in the BAPI interface, even if they exist in the underlying CDS view.

Regards, 
Gauthier VICTOR

entry.content:

I have done the ALRTCATDEF setup in my MDG system. In the action FM I am filling my return_tab with error messages.
If return_tab has error messges then AIF alert is triggered and also mail is triggered. The mail is configured in ALRTCATDEF in long/short message text tab. I am using that and only specified message filed with multiple lines as continuous text (&messages[].message[]&).

But when I get mail, whole bapiret2 structure also comes in mail.
Example: <msg_type> <msg_class> <msg_num> <message> <msg_v1> <msg_v2> etc.

(E ZMSG_CLS 009 Error in CR Request Error in CR Request)

But expectation is just the <message> since I added only the message field (&messages[].message[]&) like: Error in CR Request.

Did someone encountered such issue or this is just standard and SAP processes all the fields of return_tab?

If not please help me configure it correctly. Please fell free to ask any inputs.

Thanks,
Rahul

entry.title: Re: Additional Fields required in Asset master
entry.content:
Dear @S002476221520,

In Public Cloud the supported approach is to add these as custom fields in Manage Fixed Assets. SAP documents custom-field support for Fixed Asset Master Data (FAA_ASSET_MASTER_DATA) and Fixed Asset Master Time-Dependent Data (FAA_ASSET_MASTER_TD_DATA) and the standard asset master structure includes areas such as General and Origin

 

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/d005b509884040b18a9d6c092082a325.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4fc8d03390c342da8a60f8ee387bca1a/5c424437bf794f809087fdce391149f2.html

Recommended mapping:

  • Brand, Model Number, Custodian custom fields in Fixed Asset Master Data shown in General Data

  • Invoice Number custom field in Fixed Asset Master Data, shown in Origin
  • Building, Floor custom fields in Fixed Asset Master Time-Dependent Data, shown with the time-dependent location information

The only point to validate in the tenant is the exact UI subsection placement. SAP supports adding and rearranging fields and groups with Adapt UI but the final position within a specific subsection should be confirmed during configuration

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Goods movement not automatically process when cancell...
entry.content:
Dear @_yingbirong76d707,

In Public Cloud you cannot make CO13 post the reversal goods movements synchronously in the same online step. SAP states that for cancellation of a production order confirmation the automatic goods receipt/backflush reversal is always performed in the update program the update runs independently of the online process the lock issue can happen and this cannot be avoided

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3336586

This is also consistent with SAP confirmation configuration logic SAP documents that process control timing can be set for processes triggered by a confirmation such as automatic goods receipt, backflushing and actual cost determination. But for the CO13 cancellation case SAP separately and explicitly says the reversal posting is still handled in the update program so there is no supported Public Cloud setting that overrides CO13 into fully synchronous posting

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-manufacturing-execution/configuring-confirmations-for-production-orders

The SAP-supported way to handle this is:

  1. Cancel the confirmation in CO13
  2. Schedule Schedule Posting of Predefined Confirmation Processes as a recurring background job. SAP recommends this specifically to process goods movements after confirmation or cancellation and to clean up lock-related COGI errors
  3. If something is still missing check CO14 Goods Movements Overview and then process remaining entries in COGI / CO1P / COFC. SAP documents this exact follow-up for missing goods movements after cancellation

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/4a079b7eaf4d424db8bde639b173ff7c.html

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3379004

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3066463

Summary:

In Public Cloud, CO13 reversal goods movements are standardly asynchronous and may go to COGI with CO469. The supported workaround is a frequent job for Schedule Posting of Predefined Confirmation Processes, plus exception handling in COGI/CO1P/COFC when needed

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Somos SAP Community Curitiba – Luiz Souza

@LuizSouza_cwb, obrigado pelo espaço.

Primeiramente agradecer a comunidade SAP CWB e todas as comunidades, com toda certeza, sem a comunidade, meu inicio no mundo SAP seria muito mais complexo. Li vários blogs, e vários posts tanto Brasil quando mundo, que me ajudaram muito a chegar em resultados de algumas soluções, então incentivo muito as pessoas a permanecerem alimentando a comunidade. 

Sobre os eventos, acho eles incríveis e com muita troca de conhecimento e networking. Eles são fundamentais para as pessoas criarem conexões, trocar conhecimento e se conectarem, por exemplo, eu já conheci pessoas que posteriormente viraram colegas e também contato em alguns casos onde eu os indiquei para vagas.

Quem sou eu na SAP Community Curitiba?

Já participei de alguns eventos, como participante, patrocinador e speaker. Dentre todas essas visões vejo que sempre é uma grande oportunidade participar destes eventos, pois sempre trouxe uma nova conexão, um novo conhecimento ou alguma forma de troca. Gosto sempre de conversar com as pessoas, fazer novas conexões e ajudar de todas as formas que conseguir.

O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte da SAP Community Curitiba?

Principalmente o networking, acho que isso é fundamental em qualquer área profissional.

Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?

#SAPCommunityCWB
#Networking

entry.title: PCP0 — No Direct FI Document Drill-Down in ECP + S/4HANA Landscape — Confirmed Behaviour?
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

We are running SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (ECP) integrated with SAP S/4HANA for finance. Payroll posting is done successfully. But I am unable to drill down the document in PCP0 . From my research I have below findings but can you validate - Is it true?

In the ECP → S/4HANA landscape, PCP0 does NOT support direct FI document drill-down. This is a confirmed, architectural limitation — not a bug.

 The Root Cause: Two Separate Systems

In a classic on-premise HCM + co-deployed FI setup, PCP0 and FI live in the same SAP system. So when you double-click a posting document in PCP0, the system can directly navigate to the FI document (FB03) because both sit in the same application server and share the same document tables (BKPF, BSEG).

In the ECP → S/4HANA landscape:

ECP (SAP-managed cloud)          S/4HANA (Customer system)
─────────────────────────        ──────────────────────────
 PCP0 runs here                   FI documents live here
 Posting run created here         BKPF/BSEG entries here
 IDocs generated here     ──ALE──▶  FI document posted here

The payroll posting travels via ALE (IDocs) from ECP to S/4HANA. Once transferred:

  • ECP's PCP0 shows status "Documents Transferred"
  • The FI document is created in S/4HANA with a different document number
  • There is no cross-system drill-down link from PCP0 in ECP to FB03 in S/4HANA

The message you'll typically see in PCP0 confirms this architecture:

"Account assignment object check took place in logical system" "Document check for G/L account document took place in logical system"

These messages explicitly tell you the validation and posting happened in a different logical system — the drill-down can't cross that boundary.

 What You Can Do Instead (Workarounds)

Approach How Where

FB03 with reference numberUse the posting date + reference field from PCP0 to locate the document in S/4HANA's FB03S/4HANA
FBL3N / FS10NSearch by GL account + posting period in S/4HANAS/4HANA
RPCIP_SUPPORT / RPCIP_DOCUMENT_ANALYSEStandard SAP reports for payroll posting analysis with broader selection criteriaECP
PC00_M99_DKONView wage type → GL account assignments in ECP to reconcileECP
Payroll Control Center (PCC)Post-payroll monitoring in PCC shows FI posting status and errors — this is the recommended ECP-era alternativeECP Fiori

💡 Key Distinction

Scenario PCP0 FI Drill-Down

HCM on-premise + FI co-deployed (same system)✅ Works — direct navigation to FB03
HCM on-premise + FI on separate system (ALE)❌ Not available — same limitation
ECP (cloud) + S/4HANA (separate)❌ Not available — by design
SAP HCM for S/4HANA (embedded, same system)✅ Works

The bottom line: this is not a new limitation introduced by S/4HANA — it has always existed whenever HR and FI were on separate logical systems communicating via ALE. ECP customers simply always encounter it because ECP is by definition a separate managed system from the customer's S/4HANA.

 

 

Regards,

Niladri  SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll

entry.title: Re: Authentication Incorrect, Please check the SAP BTP de...
entry.content:
Hi.  I will just based this on the definition of SAP BAS (Build Application Studio).

So when somebody gets the "Authentication Incorrect" it may mean that or it may indicate a mismatch between the credentials or token defined in BTP Cockpit and what the backend system expects.

Due to BAS relies on the Web (proxy) to route the request, a correct connection check in the BTP does not always mean BAS can successfully authentication when you are fetching data or catalogs.

So for the purpose of this, I am sharing what may be found online:

  • Troubleshooting: https://help.sap.com/docs/bas/sap-business-application-studio/connectivity-troubleshooting 
  • Checklist to solve destination issues in SAP BAS - https://me.sap.com/notes/3689915 
  • Similar topics discussed in our community forums here:  https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/authentication-incorrect-to-connect-to-sap-system-via-business-application/qaq-p/12400858 

To help here, I am pasting our checklist below from what I gather internally (That checklist could also be dated).  Adjust accordingly or test accordingly.

----Checklist----

[ ] Restart the BAS Dev Space

Reasoning: BAS caches destination metadata to improve performance. If the destination configuration was modified in the BTP Cockpit recently, those changes often won't take effect until the specific Dev Space is stopped and restarted.

[ ] Verify Mandatory "WebIDE" Properties

Reasoning: For BAS to recognize and properly proxy a destination, the Additional Properties must include WebIDEEnabled = true and HTML5.DynamicDestination = true. If these are missing or set to false, the authentication handshake typically fails during the service discovery phase.

[ ] Confirm Cloud Connector Access Control

Reasoning: If the backend is on-premise, the Cloud Connector must explicitly allow the URL path being called (e.g., /sap/opu/odata). Even with correct credentials, a restricted "Access Control" list in the Cloud Connector can trigger a generic authentication error.

[ ] Test Technical User in the Backend System

Reasoning: If using BasicAuthentication, the credentials must be valid in the target system (e.g., SAP S/4HANA). Manually log into the backend WebGUI using the same username and password to ensure the account isn't locked, expired, or requiring a password change.

[ ] Run the "Open Environment Check"

Reasoning: This is a built-in diagnostic tool within BAS (found via the Command Palette). It performs a live test of your connections and provides specific logs that clarify if the failure is happening at the Network level, the Proxy level, or the Provider level.

[ ] Check Principal Propagation Mapping

Reasoning: If using OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion, the user’s email or ID in the BTP Identity Provider must exactly match the user mapped in the backend system. A mismatch here is the most common cause of "Authentication Incorrect" for SSO setups.

[ ] Validate Destination Name Case-Sensitivity

Reasoning: BAS is sensitive to the naming of the destination. If the project configuration file (like ui5.yaml) references a destination name that differs in casing from the one in the BTP Cockpit, the authentication lookup will fail to find the correct configuration.

-----end----------------

That is what I found and hope this helps out.

Regards,
Mark

entry.title: Implementing Labor Management in SAP EWM: From Configuration to Reporting
entry.content:
H1.0: Abstract & Introduction:

Labor Management in SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) enables organizations to define, determined and increase the workforce performance in overall warehouse operations. This blog populates end-to-end design, customization, master data setup and Testing steps required to setup the Labor Management. This encompass both direct task and Indirect tasks, how they are setup, processed and analyzed using activity area, resources and ELS (engineered labor resources. This document works as bible to design and implement labor management process in a complex warehouse.

Example of executed workload for a specific day for multiple resources: 

As per my prior experience, with below reasons, labor management implementations fail in practice:

  • Engineered Labor Standards (ELS) – The planned data was not practical or accurate, as it was copied from other sites instead of being based on real operations.
  • Warehouse Team not aligned – Warehouse persons were not taken into confidence before implementing Labor Management.
  • Incorrect KPIs – Only speed (planned time) was considered, while other important parameters such as productivity and quality were ignored.
  • Perception of punishment – Warehouse personnel believed the system would be used by top management to punish them 

Note: Outbound process will be designed same way as Inbound process(mentioned in this blog)

H2.0: Detailed process:

This blog is made for below business scenario:

  • There will be Direct tasks (WO) and Indirect tasks (Meeting, maintenance activities etc.) both.
  • Direct tasks need determined for Put away external steps (staging area 9010)
  • Performance needs to be determined for Direct task aggregate for a day
  • Workload needs to be triggered, if WO weight is less than 50 KG or more than 50 KG.
  • Planned duration would be fixed for less than 50 KG and if it is more then, planned duration time would the doubled.

H2.1: Customization:

H2.1.1: Activate Labor Management + Activate Internal process steps

Under this step, labor management needs to be activated at warehouse level, which is one of the first and mandatory step.

This step activates Labor Management for internal warehouse processes by assigning relevant process steps (e.g., PICK, PUT, PAC, INDL). Each step is enabled for performance tracking, and the workload for RF warehouse orders is defined to ensure accurate labor measurement and reporting.

* INDL is internal process step for Indirect labor task.

H2.1.2: Define External Process steps: This configuration maps external storage process steps to internal process steps used in Labor Management. Indirect activities such as meetings and maintenance are linked to the internal step INDL, ensuring proper tracking of non-operational work.

 And I have seen, this configuration is sometimes missed by many consultants. This activity must be defined properly and assigned to external steps for indirect activities such as meetings and maintenance.

H2.1.3: Define Activity: Warehouse activities are defined and categorized according to process types such as stock removal, put away, internal movements, and physical inventory. These activity definitions are critical for assigning engineered labor standards (ELS) and enabling accurate labor tracking.

H2.1.4: Assigned Plan activity area to Storage types: In this configuration, planning activity areas are assigned to specific storage types. This mapping is essential for Labor Management, as it allows the system to calculate workload, assign resources, and evaluate performance based on defined operational zones.

H2.1.5: Activation of ODM Components for Indirect Labor Tasks: ODM is a framework that enables for tracking and processing for order related data for labor management and EWL (Executed workload). Order document types are WO, WT, Internal documents and Dependent objects are Activities, resource and execution Data. Order data area is really logical grouping of data, which is used for Labor management and EWL processing.  

H2.1.6: Determine the number range for Indirect labor: Number range defined for Indirect labor task.

H2.1.7:Number range interval needs to be created for assignment of number range.

H2.1.8: Determined engineered labor standard:

Direct ELS values are maintained for warehouse process steps by defining conditions, constants, time units, and formulas. These settings are critical for calculating standard execution times and enabling accurate labor performance measurement. For each step, there are three records:

Record #1: External step 17PUN, planned time is 7 MIN

Record#2: if CON1 is satisfied (means if workload weight is <50 KG) then planned time would be 7 MIN

Record#3: If CON2 is satisfied(means if workload weight is >50 KG) then planned time would be 2x7=14 MIN

H2.1.9 : Conditions for Engineered Labor Standards: /N/SCWM/LM_CE

H2.1.9.1 :Condition CON1 is created, if workload weight is < 50 KG

H2.1.9.2 : Condition CON2 is created, if workload weight is > 50 KG

* will be used in above to change the mode as expert mode and then only you can add/change the values. And Field name can be picked from #1 and operator from #2 and Number/constant from #3.

H2.1.9.2 Formula for Engineered Labor Standards: /N/SCWM/LM_FE(entering the formula also, will have same process as above mentioned for conditions)

H2.1.9.2.1 Created FOR1, if the weight is < 50 then planned time would be 7 MIN(defined at record #1)

H2.1.9.2.2 Created FOR2, if the weight is > 50 then planned time would be 14 MIN ( 2 X defined at record#1)

H2.1.10 : Activate Change Documents in Labor Management

In this step, specific Labor Management activities are selected for change document activation. This ensures that any updates to processor data, performance documents, and time and attendance are recorded for audit and traceability purposes.

H2.1.10.1: Activate change Documents for processor

By activating this option, the system records all changes made to processor-related data. This improves transparency and supports auditing and troubleshooting within Labor Management processes.

H2.1.11 : Processor: In this configuration, processor types, reporting groups, and work categories are defined to structure labor resources. These elements are critical for capturing labor activities, grouping performance data, and enabling accurate reporting in Labor Management. Proper configuration also allows differentiation between workforce categories, supports KPI-based performance evaluation, and enhances visibility into operational efficiency across warehouse functions.

H2.1.11.1 Define Processor type:

This configuration is essential for reporting purposes, as it defines how labor resources are categorized and analyzed within the system. In this step, processor types are defined to distinguish between different categories of labor, such as internal employees and external (contract) workers.

This classification plays a critical role in accurate labor tracking, as it enables the system to capture and report activities based on workforce type. It also supports detailed performance analysis, allowing businesses to compare productivity across different labor groups.

Furthermore, processor type differentiation is important for cost analysis, as internal and external resources often have different cost structures. By maintaining this configuration correctly, organizations can achieve better visibility into workforce performance, optimize resource utilization, and make more informed operational decisions.

H2.1.11.2 Define Reporting Groups for Processors: 

Under this step, processors are grouped into reporting categories (e.g., inbound and outbound). These groups are used to consolidate labor data and support detailed performance reporting and analysis within SAP EWM Labor Management.

By organizing processors into logical reporting groups, the system enables aggregation of labor activities at a functional level, allowing businesses to analyze productivity across different warehouse operations. This helps in identifying bottlenecks, comparing performance between inbound and outbound processes, and improving operational efficiency.

Additionally, reporting groups provide a structured foundation for KPI analysis, enabling managers to monitor workload distribution, resource utilization, and process efficiency. This classification also supports better decision-making by offering clear visibility into how labor resources are performing across different operational areas.

H2.1.11.3 Define Work Categories: 

This configuration is also used for reporting purposes, where work categories are defined to classify different types of labor activities. In this scenario, three work categories are maintained—such as floor operations, back office, and support—to clearly distinguish between operational and non-operational tasks.

This classification helps in separating direct and indirect labor, enabling more accurate performance tracking and reporting. It also allows businesses to analyze productivity based on the nature of work and improve resource allocation across different functional areas.

H2.1.12 Shift Management: 

Shift management is used to define and manage workforce schedules within the warehouse. In this step, shift timings, break schedules, and capacity variants are configured to reflect actual working conditions. This enables the system to accurately determine labor availability, calculate utilization rates, and evaluate performance.

Proper shift configuration ensures that labor activities are measured within the correct time framework, improving the accuracy of KPIs and workforce planning. It also helps in aligning resource allocation with operational demand, thereby enhancing overall warehouse efficiency.

H2.1.12.1 Define Capacity Variants: 

Capacity variants are configured to represent different levels of workforce availability, such as minimum and normal capacity. This setup allows the system to plan and allocate resources dynamically based on changing operational demands.

By using capacity variants, organizations can handle scenarios such as reduced staffing, peak workload periods, or standard operating conditions. This flexibility improves workforce utilization, supports efficient resource planning, and ensures better alignment between labor availability and warehouse activities.

H2.1.12.2 Maintain Shift Management Defaults:

In this step, default values for shift management are defined, including resource capacity variants and applicable working days. These defaults provide a baseline for workforce scheduling and ensure consistency across planning activities.

By maintaining these settings, the system can automatically determine labor availability, support accurate scheduling, and align workforce capacity with operational requirements. This helps improve planning efficiency, reduces manual effort, and ensures reliable performance calculations.

H2.2:  Master data:

H2.2.1: Create a Processor - T Code: /N/SCMB/PRR1

In this step, a Business Partner (e.g., #50000283) is created and assigned the role of a warehouse worker (LM0001). This setup is essential for representing labor resources within SAP EWM Labor Management.

Key attributes such as labor factor, supply chain unit, and warehouse assignment are maintained to ensure that labor activities are captured accurately. These attributes play a critical role in calculating workload, measuring performance, and enabling detailed reporting.

By correctly configuring the processor, the system can track individual worker performance, support resource planning, and provide meaningful insights into workforce productivity.

This is one of the main master data, so without this none of labor management activities will work at all.

H2.2.2: Assign User to BP created - /SCWM/MON

In this step, the SAP user is assigned to the Business Partner representing the warehouse worker. This assignment is essential to link system user activities with the corresponding processor in SAP EWM Labor Management.

By establishing this connection, the system can accurately capture labor activities performed by the user and associate them with the correct processor. It also enables users to analyze processor-level data, including workforce classification, reporting group, and system assignment.

This master data plays a key role in monitoring labor execution, ensuring data accuracy, and supporting detailed performance evaluation and reporting.

H2.2.3 Maintain Shifts: /SCWM/SHIFT

In this master data step, warehouse shifts are configured by defining start and end times, break durations, and shift factors. These parameters form the foundation for workforce scheduling and are critical for accurate labor tracking and performance measurement.

For example, a shift (e.g., 1017_A) is created with clearly defined working hours. This ensures that all labor activities are recorded within the correct time frame and aligned with actual operational schedules.

These settings are essential for accurate labor planning, time tracking, and performance evaluation in Labor Management. Maintained the shift name as 1017_A with starting time and end timings. 

H2.2.4 : Maintain processor work schedule: /SCWM/SHIFT_WS

In this step, work schedules are assigned to processors by defining valid date ranges and corresponding shift patterns. This configuration ensures that each warehouse worker’s availability is accurately maintained in the system.

For example, a work schedule is created for processor 50000283 for the next six months, aligning the worker with predefined shifts. This allows the system to:

  • Accurately determine workforce availability
  • Calculate utilization and productivity metrics
  • Ensure labor activities are recorded within valid working periods

 

H2.3: Test Scenarios:

H2.3.1: Scenario 1: Weight is less than 50 KG

H2.3.1.1:  Inbound delivery 180107832(Created with tcode VL31N) with Handling unit(pallet)

H2.3.1.2: Warehouse order 1445(created using /SCWM/PRDI)

1: Plan Activity area 9010 is assigned to storage type 9010 under Labor management

2: 17UN is assigned to external step process step under labor management

3: Because of above two steps Labor management is active

4: As per determined engineered labor standard, condition CON1(weight < 50), planned delivery is 7 Min.

H2.3.1.3: Planned load(/SCWM/MON)

As soon as WO is created, planned load will be populating and can be seen under labor management.

H2.3.1.4: Assigned WO to Resource/User S4EWM77

Done with /SCWM/MON

H2.3.1.5: Confirm WO: using /SCWM/RFUI or /SCWM/MON

H2.3.1.6: Executed workload:

After WO confirmation, executed workload can be viewed in labor management under /SCWM/MON

1: Actual duration = 20.53

2: Adj Planned = 7

3: efficiency = (7 / 20.53) X 100 = 34.09

H2.3.2: Scenario 2: Weight is greater than 50 KG

H2.3.2.1 : Inbound delivery: 

Created with tcode VL31N with Handling unit(pallet)

H2.3.2.2 Warehouse order 1446(created using /SCWM/PRDI)

H2.3.2.3 :  Planned load(/SCWM/MON)

As soon as WO is created, planned load will be populating and can be seen under labor management.

1: As per determined engineered labor standard, condition CON1(weight > 50), planned delivery is 2 * 7 = 14 Min.

H2.3.2.4 : WO is assigned resource and confirmed Done with /SCWM/MON

H2.3.2.5 : Executed workload: 

After WO confirmation, executed workload can be viewed in labor management under /SCWM/MON

1: Actual duration = 31

2: Adj Planned = 14

3: efficiency = (14 / 31) X 100 = 45.16

Scenario#3 : Indirect Labor:

If warehouse operator is not doing direct task (WO) and having some meeting or maintenance activity, which are not planned, then this scenario will be in scope. This can be created with or without activity area. Below was created without activity area. Under this task, they would be punching start and end time. This also comes to picture when evaluating the performance of a warehouse operator.

H2.3.4: Scenario4: Executed workload

This report can be viewed in labor management under /SCWM/MON

 

H2.3.5: Scenario 5: Overall efficiency(/SCWM/SAPLWIP_EWRKL)

Aggregate efficiency can be populated using this report and can also be viewed by No aggregation, Date and activity area, Date proc and activity, External process steps, Date entitled and steps, Date and reference, Date and processor.

1: Sum of two direct tasks 10170000001445 + 10170000001445

Actual direct task time = 20 + 31 = 51

Planned direct task time = 07 + 14 = 21

Efficiency = (21/51)*100 = 40.75

2: Indirect Task

Planned=Actual task time = 17

Efficiency = (17/17) * 100=100

H3.0 Conclusion:

The implementation of Labor Management in SAP EWM provides a powerful framework for designing, result capturing and finally optimizing labor workforce performance in warehouse operations. By adding detailed steps in configuration, master data, execution, testing, and reporting, it can achieve greater transparency in labor utilization.

Proper use of planning activity areas engineered labor standards(ELS), and resource-based execution, SAP EWM enables accurate measurement of both direct and indirect activities. As demonstrated in this article, a structured and well-configured Labor Management solution can significantly enhance the operational efficiency and decision-making capabilities. For SAP consultants, this will be a very good document to simulate the process, design and implement labor management process in warehouse in an excellent way.

This is very critical process in SAP EWM and can also integrated in Human Resource department (HRD), wherein performance report can be shared with HRD team, if this is not calculated correctly then warehouse wage/salary will be impacted. ELS should well determine the planned task time with proper studies (Time and motion study) and before submitting the performance report with HRD, this needs to be well verified. 

Important considerations:

  • Incase internet is not good then will slow down the process and will delay the actual time and will impact the performance.
  • Planned time is not correct, then also performance will be impacted.
  • As this is very critical process and impact salary for warehouse person, so should be careful designed and well checked before finalizing this process.
  • Material should have correct weight, especially when a new material master is created

I would advise to consultant approaching this for the first time, should do the following:

  • Obtain practical planned data from the business and ensure that it is realistic.
  • Encourage the business to involve warehouse team from day one so that they are fully engaged. This will help build their confidence in the process.
  • Work closely with the business team to define appropriate KPIs, ensuring they gain the trust of warehouse team.
    Clearly communicate to warehouse team that the system is not intended for punishment, but for improving warehouse processes.

Incorrect ELS time estimation caused a real business problem because planned data was simply copied from other sites. And this will create so many problems to warehouse personnel like under performing, Morale decreased and finally lost the confidence in Labor management.

 

Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMCORP etc. He worked on various type of project flavor like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

entry.title: Re: SAP Fiori List report app does not load automatically...
entry.content:
Hello.  Some of our documented solutions are:

  • 3657658 Object page navigation does not trigger in custom analytical list report  (https://me.sap.com/notes/3657658 )
  • 2905853 Need to press Go again after navigating back from another app into List Report (https://me.sap.com/notes/2905853 )

If those solutions are limited, the team can use the definition of what we know which on newer system like SAPUI5 1.114.+ (deprecated by the way), the standard manifest settings for overridden by the underlying template (Adaptation) logic or the routing state.

The team may check the initialLoad property in the manifest as that influences the first Go clicked.

The team can also check if your adaptation code may have some custom logic in the onAfterRendering or onPageNavigation that may be stopping any of the rebind from occurring.

Most of the techniques above you will need to review the HAR file trace and check any of those keywords above. 

That is what I can search for now tied to what we have in our database.

Have a great day.

Regards,
Mark

entry.title: Re: handle dynamic field control
entry.content:
Hi Thomas,

You’re absolutely right — dynamic field control becomes unmanageable when every new business rule adds another IF/ELSE branch in PBO. I’ve been in the same situation, especially with table controls.

I eventually settled on a pattern that keeps the PBO clean by externalizing all field rules into a configuration table + screen groups, and then using one single LOOP AT SCREEN to apply the rules.
This way, the PBO never grows — only the config does.

How I solved it
Assign every field to a SCREEN-GROUP1 (e.g., GR1, GR2, MAND, HIDE).

Maintain a small internal table of rules based on scenario, document type, or user action.

Apply all rules in one LOOP AT SCREEN, without hardcoding field names.

Example Code (clean, scalable)


TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_rule,
group TYPE screen-group1,
input TYPE c,
required TYPE c,
invisible TYPE c,
END OF ty_rule.

DATA: lt_rules TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_rule,
ls_rule TYPE ty_rule.

" Build rules dynamically based on scenario
IF gv_scenario = 'REST'. " Residential Customer
    APPEND VALUE #( group = 'GR1' input = '1' required = '1' ) TO lt_rules.
    APPEND VALUE #( group = 'SADD' invisible = '0' ) TO lt_rules.
ELSEIF gv_scenario = 'IND'. " Industrial customer
   APPEND VALUE #( group = 'SADD' invisible = '1' ) TO lt_rules.
ELSE.
   APPEND VALUE #( group = 'GR2' input = '1' required = '1' ) TO lt_rules.
ENDIF.

" Apply rules in ONE place
MODULE pbo_field_control OUTPUT.
LOOP AT SCREEN.
READ TABLE lt_rules INTO ls_rule WITH KEY group = screen-group1.
IF sy-subrc = 0.
IF ls_rule-input IS NOT INITIAL.
screen-input = ls_rule-input.
ENDIF.
IF ls_rule-required IS NOT INITIAL.
screen-required = ls_rule-required.
ENDIF.
IF ls_rule-invisible IS NOT INITIAL.
screen-invisible = ls_rule-invisible.
ENDIF.
MODIFY SCREEN.
ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.
ENDMODULE.

This approach kept my module pool maintainable even when the business added multiple new scenarios over time. 

My problem solve with this approach and based on customer type my screen fields getting populated for user input.

entry.title: Re: Event-driven architecture
entry.content:
Thanks for pointing out Advanced Event Mesh — I agree it ticks a lot of boxes on the infrastructure side: multi‑consumer fan‑out, routing, filtering, replay, and hybrid connectivity are much stronger there than with standard Event Mesh.

For me, AEM solves the event backbone problem (distribution, durability, routing at scale), but I still separate that from the consumer boundary, where I need: – business logic and orchestration – enrichment and validation – idempotency and error handling – coordination across multiple downstream systems

In other words, I see AEM as the enterprise event fabric, and CAP/iFlows as domain consumers that sit on top of it.

So my current thinking is: – AEM for large‑scale, multi‑consumer, hybrid event distribution – CAP or iFlows when I need domain logic, composition, or API exposure on top of those events.

Curious how you’ve drawn that boundary in your projects—do you let AEM do as much as possible, or do you still keep a thin application/service layer for domain behavior?

entry.title: Re: Event-driven architecture
entry.content: At the end of the day, AEM or IS are integration layers. Not supposed to carry business logic. Technical look ups, transformation and translation, yes. But building business logic in the integration layer would become a nightmare. What I do not under stand in your usecase is, when you say multi consumer, would you be able to explain, may be with PizzaHut kind of an example or something totally different? The consumers are always expected to applications or instances that would have the functional/business logic built and then take appropriate actions. All you need is transaction postings in various backend system then we are talking integration platform. Just trying to understand the details.

entry.title: Employees who don't belong to the company are listed in the history reports.
entry.content:
Greetings,

I am writing to share the following issue with history reports:

In the production environment, a history report is being generated, but it is showing two users who don't belong to the company, as shown in the following image.

A search is performed in the employee's profile, and the result is as expected: the users don't exist and are not showing up as inactive, as shown in the following image:

I look forward to your help in determining the source of this unusual incident.

Sincerely,

entry.title: DebugSheet: Get XML Data for Local Print Form Preview in the Adobe LiveCycle Designer
entry.content:
Overview of my Print Form Resources

I decided to create a separate article to avoid duplicate content in each print form blog post and simplify the maintenance. You can find the list of print form-related Blogs, information on Live Sessions and an Onboarding Video for beginners in the following blog post:

Print Form Adaptation with Adobe LiveCycle Designer Overview - Onboarding, Blogs and Live Session

 

Intro

When working on print forms in Adobe LiveCycle Designer, having XML data (sometimes called sample data) to preview the form locally is invaluable. Here are some of the key benefits:

  • No need to upload to preview: You can check your current layout and verify that adjustments render correctly — all without uploading the form and opening the target business object in the system again and again after each little adjustment.
  • Field identification: With real sample data from your system, you can compare the output directly to the system screen and locate fields by their content, which is especially helpful when you don’t know a field’s exact technical name.
  • Local data manipulation: You can modify data locally — extend a text, insert line breaks, duplicate items, and so on — to see how the form behaves with different data sets.

Bottom line: Nothing beats real sample data from the target system. 💪

 

How to get XML Data in SAP systems?

The ways to obtain that XML data vary significantly across SAP solutions and often even differ by business object within the same solution. This guide applies to SAP solutions where forms are rendered via the Adobe Document Services (ADS) and customized using Adobe LiveCycle Designer. These include:

  • SAP Sales/Service Cloud (Version 1, formerly SAP Cloud for Customer)
  • SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • SAP Business ByDesign

The approach described here is often a last resort, as it involves some effort — if a system offers better ways, use those 😉
However, the DebugSheet provides an unfiltered look behind the scenes.

Important: Since this method requires replacing the currently active form template in the system, it should never be used in production environments!

 

Procedure

  1. Copy the content of the XML code block from the end of this blog into a text editor and save the file as DebugSheet2.xdp
    This workaround is necessary because uploading .xdp files is not supported here.
  2. Next, upload the file as the template for the desired document in your system. Here’s where to do that in each solution:
    • SAP Sales/Service Cloud: Work Center Administrator → View Form Template Maintenance
    • SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2: System Settings → Form Templates → (All Settings) → (Output Management) → Maintain Form Template
    • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Output Management Form Template app
    • SAP Business ByDesign: Work Center Application and User Management → View Form Template Maintenance

  3. Don’t forget to publish/activate the template and, if applicable, configure any rules that ensure the template is actually used for output.
  4. Now navigate to the relevant business object or app in the system and trigger the PDF generation.
    The DebugSheet will appear, displaying the XML data.

 

A word of caution when copying: Copying text from PDF files is generally not as seamless as copying from, say, a Word document. For example, selecting and copying text across multiple pages in a PDF often pulls in unwanted content from headers, footers, or other overlapping elements. The DebugSheet uses a layout that is optimized for copying XML content spanning multiple pages, but you may still encounter issues with truncated or wrapped lines. At the very least, however, you can search for specific fields and their values to verify whether certain values are part of the data set at all and could therefore be displayed in the PDF.

 

Behind the Scenes

Extracting the XML data is no rocket science. The DebugSheet essentially leverages a built-in function and outputs the result in an optimized layout. The following line of JavaScript does the heavy lifting:

this.rawValue = xfa.data.saveXML("pretty");

Here, this should reference a text field that allows line breaks and whose height expands automatically to fit the content.

 

DebugSheet2.xdp

Copy the code block's content to a text editor and save it as DebugSheet2.xdp. This workaround is necessary because uploading .xdp files is not supported here. (Do not use Word or similar programs, since they don't save plain text files.)

entry.title: Range Table at Cockpit
entry.content:
Hi guys!

I am facing a issue working with range table at Cockpit.
As a tried to show in the images attached, I am not understanding the behavior, and I also can't see what I am possible doing rong.

On a Bounded range table, searching for a range to a value belongs to the 1º range, the compenent returs the result that belongs to the last range. This behavior continues till I send a value over the last boundary, when the component returs the value that belongs to the 1º range.

In another test, using a Unbounded range table the component is returning always the result that belongs to the 1º range.

I couldn't try the example in Core Tool because an error trying to save the range table class.

Thanks

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Code – Technical Series – Feature Flags Service
entry.content:
correct me if I am wrong but the CAP feature toggles is different

what if, our CAP applications does not support multi-tenancy and we don't have any plan to support it

the CAP feature toggle is at the request level and can't be operated via the SAP BTP Feature Flags application subscription

what about this community plugins?

https://github.com/cap-js-community/feature-toggle-library?tab=readme-ov-file

if we would like to use the SAP BTP Feature Flags application subscription

we need to create a service instance of Feature Flags and bind it to our service via MTA ?

or we consume it via the Destination Service using the SAP Cloud SDK ?

thanks

entry.title: SAP Public Cloud DRC South Korea - PDF display of eDocument not working
entry.content:
Hello guys, I'm configuring SAP DRC for South Korea. So far I am able to create eDocument for billing documents and download XML file for them. 

However, when I click 'Display PDF', the message 'The action is not allowed for any of the selected eDocuments' appear.

In 'Output Parameter Determination' app, I assigned pre-delivered Korean PDF templates for billing documents as shown below.

Also, I've created three channels for billing documents

Can any of you help me why display PDF button isn't working? I want to know what I've missed. By the way, my tenant doesn't have an app called 'Specify Output Form for PDF Generation'.

Thanks.

entry.title: ALRTCATDEF - How to change the error message format from action FM (MDG)
entry.content:
Hi all,

Kindly help to get the ALRTCATDEF message in proper format.

I have done the ALRTCATDEF setup in my MDG system. In the action FM I am filling my return_tab with error messages.

If return_tab has error messges then AIF alert is triggered and also mail is triggered. The mail is configured in ALRTCATDEF in long/short message text tab. There, I am using container object to fill the message. Container object has message with type bapiret2. I am using that and only specified message filed with multiple lines as continuous text (&messages[].message[]&).

 

But when I get mail, whole bapiret2 structure also comes in mail.

Example: <msg_type> <msg_class> <msg_num> <message> <msg_v1> <msg_v2> etc.

 

(E ZMSG_CLS 009 Error in CR Request Error in CR Request)

 

But expectation is just the <message> since I added only the message field (&messages[].message[]&) like: Error in CR Request.

 

Did someone encountered such issue or this is just standard and SAP processes all the fields of return_tab?

 

If not please help me configure it correctly. Please fell free to ask any inputs.

 

Thanks,

Rahul

entry.title: What Is SAP Enterprise Service Management and Why It Matters Now
entry.content:
Service expectations have changed.

An employee needs help with payroll. A supplier has a billing question. A partner needs fast approval. Each one expects the same speed and clarity they get as consumers. When service lives in separate tools and teams, delays follow and frustration grows.

That’s where SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) fits.

SAP Enterprise Service Management helps organizations deliver fast, consistent service across the enterprise not just for customers, but for employees, suppliers, and partners too.

 

What Is SAP Enterprise Service Management?

SAP Enterprise Service Management is SAP’s enterprise-wide, omnichannel service solution. It standardizes how service is delivered across areas like HR, finance, procurement, facilities, and shared services.

Instead of juggling siloed ticketing tools, teams work from one AI-enabled experience from inquiry to resolution. The result is clearer ownership, faster outcomes, and lower operational effort.

 

Is SAP Enterprise Service Management Part of SAP Service Cloud Version 2?

This question comes up often. The short answer is yes and no.

SAP Enterprise Service Management is built on the same modern foundation as SAP Service Cloud Version 2, but it serves a different purpose.

Both solutions share:

  • A modern case management framework
  • A unified agent workspace
  • Embedded AI
  • Native Microsoft Teams integration
  • Deep integration with SAP back-end systems

Where they differ is who they serve.

  • SAP Service Cloud Version 2 focuses on external customer service (B2B and B2C).
  • SAP Enterprise Service Management supports internal and enterprise services for employees, suppliers, partners, and shared service teams.

Many organizations run both side by side. Together, they create one service operating model inside and out.

 

Is SAP Enterprise Service Management a New Solution?

SAP Enterprise Service Management isn’t an experiment. It’s the next step in SAP's approach to enterprise service delivery.

SAP has supported HR and shared services for years. ESM is the first solution designed from the start to manage enterprise-wide service at scale.

It includes:

  • Pre-delivered service scenarios
  • Configurable case models
  • AI-driven automation
  • Tight integration with SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, and other SAP solutions

We continue to invest heavily in ESM, with quarterly innovation and growing adoption, including across SAP’s own internal service teams.

 

How SAP Enterprise Service Management Works with SAP CX

SAP Enterprise Service Management extends the SAP Customer Experience portfolio. It doesn’t replace it.

Together, SAP CX and ESM support:

  • End-to-end service orchestration across front and back office
  • Consistent experiences for customers and employees
  • Shared insights across service domains
  • Reuse of AI, automation, and workflows

For example, a customer case in SAP Service Cloud Version 2 can trigger a finance or procurement request handled in SAP ESM. Agents work in familiar tools, while users access services through simple self-service entry points.

The outcome is scale without creating new silos.

 

How Organizations Benefit from SAP Enterprise Service Management

Customers typically see value in four areas.

Centralized Service Delivery

Enterprise inquiries across HR, finance, IT, and procurement are managed in one system. Visibility improves, and handoffs become easier.

Faster Resolution with AI

Embedded AI supports case classification, routing, summarization, and knowledge recommendations. Teams spend less time on manual work and close cases faster.

Better Employee and Partner Experiences

Self-service portals, guided forms, and omnichannel access reduce friction and increase satisfaction for everyone involved.

Lower Total Cost of Ownership

Pre-delivered content and native SAP integrations reduce custom development and speed time to value.

 

Get Started with the SAP Enterprise Service Management TKB

If you want to explore further, the SAP Enterprise Service Management Technical Knowledge Base (TKB) is a strong starting point.

The TKB helps you:

  • Understand solution positioning and value
  • Review implementation guidance and best practices
  • Explore architecture and integration options
  • Stay current with innovations and releases

It supports the full journey from discovery to optimization.

Final Thoughts

SAP Enterprise Service Management helps organizations rethink service. Instead of disconnected tickets, service becomes an intelligent, connected experience across the enterprise.

Built on the same foundation as SAP Service Cloud Version 2 and aligned with SAP CX, ESM supports faster, smarter, and more consistent service wherever it happens.

If you’re ready to extend service excellence beyond customer support and into the core of the business, SAP Enterprise Service Management is designed to help you get there.

entry.title: Re: Can I simulate actions in JS for external services for complex back-end logic?
entry.content:
Hi Martin,

This quick example is very basic (and note - if I create json files, the service mocks this fine):

extending the admin-service.cds from the sample:

using {sap.capire.bookshop as my} from '../db/schema';
using {API_BUSINESS_PARTNER as external} from './external/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER';
service AdminService {
  entity Customer as projection on external.A_BusinessPartner;
  entity Authors as projection on my.Authors;
  entity Books   as projection on my.Books;
  entity Genres  as projection on my.Genres;
}

Note - I don't want to modify the admin-service.js file for this service, as this is about a mock framework to run in development mode, so code should be identical to production code. 

That said, possibly my mock.js is the issue as it looks like this (and just trying to figure if it works yet rather than do anything) - e.g. It could be trying to load it, maybe needing the cds.connect.to('API_BUSINESS_PARTNER') - and is that what you are referring to?

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function() {
	const { A_BusinessPartner } = this.entities;

	this.on('READ', A_BusinessPartner, request => {
		return null;
	});
});

 Thanks,

Matt

entry.title: Get XML Data for Local Print Form Preview in SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2
entry.content:
Overview of my Print Form Resources

I decided to create a separate article to avoid duplicate content in each print form blog post and simplify the maintenance. You can find the list of print form-related Blogs, information on Live Sessions and an Onboarding Video for beginners in the following blog post:

Print Form Adaptation with Adobe LiveCycle Designer Overview - Onboarding, Blogs and Live Session

 

Intro

Previewing print forms "locally" within the Adobe LiveCycle Designer is an essential feature. Otherwise, you spend too much time uploading the print form to the system to preview it online after every small change. However, not only changing the template and testing it straight away is a significant advantage. Simply adapting the XML sample data to see, for instance, how a field looks when containing a different value, longer text, etc., is beneficial in contrast to creating a new sample document within the system for this purpose.

If you are looking for details on this topic for SAP Sales/Service Cloud version 1 (formerly known as "Cloud for Customer"), you should have a look at this blog post: How to get XML data for local preview of print forms without output history.

 

REST API Approach

SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2 heavily relies on REST APIs, for instance, to load object data in the web frontend. You can see this in the browser's Developer Tools / Network monitor. In general, the object structure provided by these services is compatible with the form template sample data. For Sales Quotes, there is a special parameter that returns additional data (pricing elements with pre-calculated totals) for the output.

There are two challenges:

  1. Find the right service endpoint and object id
  2. The response of the REST API is in JSON format, whereas the Adobe LiveCycle Designer requires XML

I'll solve both challenges in this blog.

 

Tools - Prerequisites

As part of the procedure, we must convert JSON to XML. This is also mentioned in the SAP Note / Knowledgebase Article 3620996 (Sales Quote XML – How to Retrieve JSON Data for Conversion in Sales Cloud Version 2).
In this guide, I use Visual Studio Code (VSCode) in combination with plugin:

  •  convert json, yaml, xml, and others (petli-full.json-to-yaml-and-more)

Visual Studio Code is free and available for all common platforms. The mentioned plugins are published under the MIT license, and their source code can be found on Git Hub.
Never use any kind of free or publicly available online services for such kinds of tasks due to data protection and privacy reasons!

 

 

Procedure

Log in to SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2 with your browser and navigate to the screen from where you can open the desired business object.

Browser Developer Tools

Open your browser's Developer Tools and activate the Network tab. In most browsers they look very similar and offer the same feature set. Depending on your browser, some elements might have a different caption. The screenshots of this blog were created using the Windows version of Google Chrome. In many browsers you can also access the developer tools by pressing [F12] or the key combination [Ctrl]+[Shift]+[ i ].

In Chrome, there are two buttons on the very left (A) that allow you to start and stop recording network activities and clear the log in case you have recorded too much and want to start over again. Make sure the recording is active while carrying out the next step.

Find the right URL

In SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2, you can now hit the button to generate the PDF. In the sales quotes, it's called Preview and can be found in the three-dots menu. In other objects, it might be called differently, such as Generate Summary, etc.

After that, the network monitor reveals the actual URL we need:

  • For the sales quotes, it'll show two calls: call with a UUID followed by ?$forOutput=true and a preview.
    Copy the URL with forOutput=true as it is.
  • For other objects, it'll only show preview. The full URL also contains a UUID and ends with /preview.
    Copy the URL of this call without the /preview at the end.

The following two screenshots show examples for sales quotes and leads.

Screenshot for Sales Quotes:

 

Screenshot for Leads:

Alternatively, you activate the network monitor right before you open the object. Then, you have to search for the call to the object itself (with its UUID) instead of a preview call. That means, when opening a lead in SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2, the Network monitor will show a call to the service /sap/c4c/api/v1/lead-service/leads/<UUID> that returns the lead data as JSON.

Retrieve the JSON Data

Paste the copied URL into the address bar of your browser. The browser will show a JSON document. (It's important that you're logged in to SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2 when accessing the copied URL. In case you have been logged out, for instance due to inactivity, the call will fail. You won't be redirected to a login screen.

 

Convert JSON to XML

Copy the JSON text into a new file in Visual Studio Code. Then, open the command palette and search for JSON, YAML, XML and more. After selecting it, you have to enter the source and the target format in a second prompt. Enter "json | xml" (without the quotes) and wait a moment. The plugin will automatically create the XML representation of the JSON file in a new document tab.

The final step is to replace the root element <root> and the XML declaration with the one for Adobe form templates:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xfa:data xmlns:xfa="http://www.xfa.org/schema/xfa-data/1.0/">
	<data>
		...
	</data>
</xfa:data>

The following screenshot shows the XML before (left) and after (right):

After replacing the root element, you have the final sample data XML that you can load into the Adobe LiveCycle Designer for local preview.

That's it!

At first, this procedure might seem a bit complex and cumbersome. But it's worth the effort! The good news is: You can do it whenever you need it without opening a support ticket and waiting for an answer, or uploading a special template, which might not be possible in a productive environment, etc.

 

Limitations

When comparing the data from the REST API response with the data that is actually used for rendering PDFs, I found out that there are differences. In my test, the REST API was missing some fields (for instance, for the sales quote: formatted address fields). In addition to that, some number/currency fields used a different number formatting.

 

Conclusion

While the data of the REST API might serve as valid XML sample data from the structural point of view, it might still differ. Hence, you cannot completely rely on this approach for the entire design and testing process, at least not without knowing the differences and where to pay attention to. However, knowing these differences can still help you quickly get sample data even if it is out of productive systems.

If you want to be sure you're looking at the exact same data as the Adobe Document Services receive when rendering the form, you should consider using my Debug Sheet, which can be found in my other blog post: DebugSheet: Get XML Data for Local Print Form Preview in the Adobe LiveCycle Designer.
